citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
APHIS Officials observed multiple coatimundis in different stages of hair loss:
• The male coatimundi in a green cage by the entrance had hair loss on the tail and rump area with the coatimundi
actively scratching all over his body. The tail and rump area had multiple sections of redness, inflammation, and scabs.
This animal never stopped scratching while the APHIS Officials were on property, and it also tried to scratch its belly
against the feeding trough. This animal also had bleeding in the area it was scratching.
• The second coatimundi in the middle area of the property had hair loss and bleeding at the tip of the tail. The animal was
seen scratching and biting on its tail.
• The third coatimundi in a separate enclosure, but close to the enclosure of the second coatimundi had approximately a
3-inch-long hair loss underneath the base of the tail.
The Program of Veterinary Care did not provide instructions for treating skin issues and no information to show the
animals condition had been observed and the information conveyed to the veterinarian.
Daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being may be accomplished by someone other than the
attending veterinarian, provided that information is conveyed to the attending veterinarian in a timely manner.
Information concerning this animal shall be conveyed to the veterinarian and appropriate treatment provided.
Correct by: February 14, 2026

At time of inspection, the licensee did not have documentation of training on the contingency plan for all personnel.
Employees must know their roles and responsibilities as outlined in the contingency plan in order to ensure animal care
and welfare in emergency events. The licensee must train personnel on the contingency plan and new employees must
be trained within 30 days of hire.
Correct by: March 12, 2026

A male Lady Amherst Pheasant, did not have an adequately sized enclosure provided that allows the bird to comfortably
hold its long tail in the enclosure. The pheasant had its tail bent and was pacing the length of the small cage it was been
held. Failure to provide an adequate spaced enclosure for full body movement and normal postural behaviors, can lead to
increased stress and a decline in physical condition. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so as to
allow each bird to make normal postural and social adjustments, such as dust-bathing and foraging, with adequate
freedom of movement and freedom to escape from aggression demonstrated by other animals. Both part-time and full-
time attending veterinarians at a facility must consult with the facility to ensure that the space in all enclosures housing
birds is adequate and allows for normal postural and social adjustments. Inadequate space may be indicated by evidence
of malnutrition, poor condition, debility, stress, or abnormal behavior patterns.
Correct by: February 26, 2026.

citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
An eclectus didn’t have any enrichment in its enclosure. It only had a food and water dish. Additionally, the facility didn’t
have an adequate environmental enhancement plan that was approved by the attending veterinarian. Failure to maintain
and follow an appropriate approved written plan can be detrimental to the psychological health and well-being of the
animals. The environment enhancement plan must be in place to promote the psychological well-being of the birds. The
licensee must provide and follow an enrichment plan that is approved by their attending veterinarian.
Correct by: February 26, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Facility Representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Sarah Roloff, CS n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
APHIS Officials observed multiple coatimundis in different stages of hair loss: 
• The male coatimundi in a green cage by the entrance had hair loss on the tail and rump area with the coatimundi

• The second coatimundi in the middle area of the property had hair loss and bleeding at the tip of the tail. The animal was 
seen scratching and biting on its tail. 
• The third coatimundi in a separate enclosure, but close to the enclosure of the second coatimundi had approximately a 
3-inch-long hair loss underneath the base of the tail. 
 
The Program of Veterinary Care did not provide instructions for treating skin issues and no information to show the 
animals condition had been observed and the information conveyed to the veterinarian. 
 
Daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being may be accomplished by someone other than the 
attending veterinarian, provided that information is conveyed to the attending veterinarian in a timely manner. 
 
Information concerning this animal shall be conveyed to the veterinarian and appropriate treatment provided.

2.134(c) 
   
Contingency planning. 
At time of inspection, the licensee did not have documentation of training on the contingency plan for all personnel. 
Employees must know their roles and responsibilities as outlined in the contingency plan in order to ensure animal care 
and welfare in emergency events. The licensee must train personnel on the contingency plan and new employees must 
be trained within 30 days of hire. 
Correct by: March 12, 2026

3.153(b) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
A male Lady Amherst Pheasant, did not have an adequately sized enclosure provided that allows the bird to comfortably 
hold its long tail in the enclosure. The pheasant had its tail bent and was pacing the length of the small cage it was been 
held. Failure to provide an adequate spaced enclosure for full body movement and normal postural behaviors, can lead to 
increased stress and a decline in physical condition. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so as to 
allow each bird to make normal postural and social adjustments, such as dust-bathing and foraging, with adequate 
freedom of movement and freedom to escape from aggression demonstrated by other animals. Both part-time and full-
time attending veterinarians at a facility must consult with the facility to ensure that the space in all enclosures housing 
birds is adequate and allows for normal postural and social adjustments. Inadequate space may be indicated by evidence 
of malnutrition, poor condition, debility, stress, or abnormal behavior patterns. 
 
Correct by: February 26, 2026. 
3.154 
   
Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being. 
An eclectus didn’t have any enrichment in its enclosure. It only had a food and water dish. Additionally, the facility didn’t 
have an adequate environmental enhancement plan that was approved by the attending veterinarian. Failure to maintain 
and follow an appropriate approved written plan can be detrimental to the psychological health and well-being of the 
animals. The environment enhancement plan must be in place to promote the psychological well-being of the birds. The 
licensee must provide and follow an enrichment plan that is approved by their attending veterinarian. 
 
Correct by: February 26, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Facility Representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Sarah Roloff, CS n
